site.title: Harness Engineering
site.tagline: Build the layer that turns a model into an agent. A pi-style coding harness, from scratch.

sections.title: Start Here
sections.blurb: The thesis. Why a model alone is not an agent, the precise difference between prompt, context, and harness engineering, and the five layers you will build across the workshop into your own coding harness.

articles.title: What is a harness?
articles.blurb: Claude Code is not a model. It is a harness. The layer of loop, tools, memory, and recovery wrapped around a model that turns transactional inference into a working agent.

articles.title: Why 'just call the API' fails
articles.blurb: A single completion is a transaction. An agent runs for two hundred turns, uses tools, recovers from errors, and remembers. The gap between them is the harness.

articles.title: Prompt vs. context vs. harness engineering
articles.blurb: Three disciplines, precise boundaries: what you say, what the model sees each turn, and the whole runtime the model lives inside. Context engineering is one subsystem of the harness.

articles.title: The anatomy: five layers of a harness
articles.blurb: The loop, tools + guardrails, the context engine, durability, and orchestration. The map for everything that follows, and for the harness you build.

articles.title: What you will build: a pi-style harness
articles.blurb: A terminal coding agent you own end to end, in the spirit of pi (pi.dev): it edits real files, asks before dangerous actions, compacts its own context, checkpoints every step, and dispatches sub-agents.

articles.title: How to use this site
articles.blurb: The two surfaces, the build-along code, and the order to read things in. How the book maps to the five live days.

sections.title: The Agent Loop
sections.blurb: Day 1. The beating heart of every harness: a loop that calls the model, runs the tools it asks for, and repeats until the work is done. We build a bare harness from first principles.

articles.title: The agent loop from first principles
articles.blurb: Call the model, see if it wants a tool, run it, feed the result back, repeat. The whole of agency is this while-loop. We derive it from nothing.

articles.title: Messages, turns & roles
articles.blurb: The conversation as an array of typed messages (system, user, assistant, tool). How a turn is structured and why the message array IS the agent's state.

articles.title: Stop conditions: when does the loop end?
articles.blurb: The model stops asking for tools, hits a limit, or is interrupted. Getting the stop condition right is the difference between an agent and an infinite loop.

articles.title: Streaming responses into a terminal
articles.blurb: Why you stream tokens and tool calls as they arrive instead of waiting for the full response, and how that changes the loop and the UI.

articles.title: The model client & provider abstraction
articles.blurb: One thin interface over the model API (and why models.json / a provider abstraction keeps the harness model-agnostic, a core pi idea).

articles.title: Build: your first bare harness
articles.blurb: Model client + message array + a hand-rolled loop that runs until the model stops asking for work. The smallest thing that is genuinely an agent.

sections.title: Tools & the Execution Environment
sections.blurb: Day 2. A harness that can only talk is a chatbot. We give it hands: file and shell tools with strict schemas, permission gates before dangerous actions, and a sandbox to contain the blast radius.

articles.title: Tool schemas as contracts
articles.blurb: A tool is a name, a JSON-schema for its arguments, and a function. The schema is the contract between the model and your code. Get it right and the model calls it correctly.

articles.title: The core tools: read, write, edit, bash, search
articles.blurb: The handful of tools a coding agent actually needs, how each is shaped, and why edit (not overwrite) and a good search tool matter more than you'd think.

articles.title: Streaming tool calls to the UI
articles.blurb: Showing the user what the agent is doing as it does it: partial tool calls, results, and the terminal UI that makes a harness feel alive and trustworthy.

articles.title: Permission gates & approval modes
articles.blurb: Why Claude Code asks before rm. Approval modes, allow-lists, and the human-in-the-loop gate that sits between a tool call and its execution.

articles.title: Sandboxing & the blast-radius problem
articles.blurb: An agent runs arbitrary commands. How to contain what a mistaken or malicious tool call can touch: sandboxes, working-directory limits, and containers.

articles.title: Code-mode vs. tool-mode
articles.blurb: Sometimes the best tool is a sandbox that runs code the model writes. When to let the model compute in a sandbox instead of calling fixed tools, and the tradeoffs.

sections.title: Context Engineering
sections.blurb: Day 3. The context window is the scarcest resource in the system. We build the machinery that decides what the model sees every turn, compacts long sessions, and remembers across them.

articles.title: The context window as a resource
articles.blurb: Attention is finite and every token costs money and focus. Context engineering is the art of deciding what enters the window each turn. The harness is the allocator.

articles.title: Context budgets: what goes in, what gets evicted
articles.blurb: Accounting for tokens: system prompt, memory, recent turns, tool results. How to budget the window and what to drop first when it fills.

articles.title: Compaction & summarization
articles.blurb: Surviving a 200-turn session without losing the plot: summarize old turns into a running summary, and hydrate it back so the agent never forgets what it's doing.

articles.title: Memory systems & the CLAUDE.md pattern
articles.blurb: Session state, persistent memory files, and the CLAUDE.md pattern: durable facts the harness loads every session so the agent starts already knowing the project.

articles.title: System prompts as infrastructure, not prose
articles.blurb: The system prompt is not a personality blurb, it is the operating manual the harness assembles every turn: tools, rules, state, and mode. Treat it like code.

articles.title: Build: the context engine
articles.blurb: Compaction plus a persistent memory layer wired into the loop, so your harness survives a long session and remembers across runs.

sections.title: Durability, Recovery & Orchestration
sections.blurb: Day 4. Real agents crash, get interrupted, and hit jobs too big for one context. We make the harness recoverable and self-healing, and teach it to dispatch sub-agents under supervision.

articles.title: Durable execution & checkpointing
articles.blurb: Wrap every model turn and tool call as a step whose result is persisted. If the process dies, replay returns the cached results instead of re-running the work.

articles.title: Replay & resumable sessions
articles.blurb: How a durable event log lets a session resume exactly where it left off after a crash, a disconnect, or a restart, with no lost work and no duplicate side effects.

articles.title: Self-healing loops: retries & failure classification
articles.blurb: Not every error is fatal. Classify failures (transient vs terminal), retry with backoff, and let the loop recover on its own instead of dying on the first hiccup.

articles.title: Sub-agents & handoffs
articles.blurb: When one context can't hold the job, spawn a focused sub-agent, let it work, and return only its conclusion. Handoffs vs. sub-agents, and when to use each.

articles.title: Supervision & plan mode
articles.blurb: A supervisor that makes a plan, dispatches sub-agents to execute steps, and synthesizes their findings. Plan-mode as a safety and quality mechanism.

articles.title: Human-in-the-loop: plans, approvals, escalation
articles.blurb: Some actions should wait for a human. Building the approval gate and escalation path, and the checkpointed dispatcher that ties durability and orchestration together.

sections.title: Production Harnesses & Capstone
sections.blurb: Day 5. We dissect three real harnesses to see how they implement, at scale, everything you just built by hand, then you assemble your own and demo it.

articles.title: pi internals: the minimal-surface philosophy
articles.blurb: How pi (pi.dev) stays tiny: extensions, models.json, and a deliberately small surface. The proof that a real harness doesn't have to be enormous.

articles.title: Hermes internals: a research lab's harness
articles.blurb: Nous Research's Hermes as a contrast: a different set of design choices for the same problems of loop, tools, memory, and control.

articles.title: Claude Code internals: skills, hooks, MCP, sub-agents
articles.blurb: The reference production harness: permission modes, hooks, skills, MCP servers, and sub-agent types, mapped onto the layers you built.

articles.title: Evaluating a harness: how you know yours works
articles.blurb: Beyond 'it ran': task success, recovery under failure, token efficiency, and safe-by-default behavior. How to actually measure a harness.

articles.title: The capstone: your own harness
articles.blurb: Assemble the loop, tools, context engine, durability, and orchestration into your own pi-style coding harness, and demo it live. Yours forever.

sections.title: Pi from Scratch
sections.blurb: How Pi actually works, built up from an empty file the way you would build any coding agent, one layer at a time — but every layer is Pi's real code (earendil-works/pi, MIT). We start with the loop and add exactly one thing per chapter, each because the last chapter left something missing: the toolbox, tool safety, the system prompt, the execution environment, context and cache, subagents, surfaces, and the extension system. Every claim is read straight from the source.

articles.title: Pi from scratch: the plan
articles.blurb: The whole of Pi on one page before we build it: the three package layers (ai / agent / coding-agent), the small-core-plus-extensions philosophy, and the order we will build it up in. Your map for the rest of the section.

articles.title: The loop: from a chat call to an agent
articles.blurb: The beating heart of Pi. How a plain model call becomes an agent: the loop calls the model, detects tool-use, runs the tools, threads the results back, and repeats until the model stops asking. Pi's real agent runtime, and why state is a session tree, not just an array.

articles.title: The toolbox: giving Pi hands
articles.blurb: A loop that can only talk is a chatbot. Pi's real tools — read, write, edit, bash, grep, find, ls (plus MCP) — and the shape of a Pi tool: name, description, a typed parameter schema, and an execute function. Why the description string is the model's real API.

articles.title: Tool safety: permission gates, protected paths, trust
articles.blurb: The first time bash runs a command you didn't expect, you understand why this chapter exists. How Pi gates dangerous tools before they execute, protects paths, and asks for project trust — all as hooks around tool execution, so safety is policy you can see.

articles.title: The system prompt: how Pi tells the model who it is
articles.blurb: Not one magic string. Pi assembles the prompt compositionally — a base template, your appended instructions, a project_context block of every AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md found walking up from the cwd, and an available_skills block — then stamps the date and working directory. Read from buildSystemPrompt().

articles.title: The execution environment: local, SSH, sandboxed
articles.blurb: Where do the tools actually run? Pi threads an operations layer through every tool so the same read/bash/edit can run on your machine, over SSH, or in a sandbox without changing the tool. The injection mechanism (ReadOperations and friends) and the blast-radius idea.

articles.title: How Pi manages context (not 'the last three messages')
articles.blurb: Pi does not keep a fixed message window. It compacts on a real token budget: trigger at contextWindow minus 16,384, keep about 20,000 tokens of recent context, and summarize everything older into a structured digest of decisions and files touched. Read straight from compaction.ts.

articles.title: How Pi does cache control: the rolling breakpoint
articles.blurb: Pi attaches an Anthropic ephemeral cache breakpoint to the last tool schema and the newest user message, a rolling breakpoint that follows the conversation so the whole growing prefix is served from cache each turn. Why a 100-turn Pi session stays cheap.

articles.title: Does Pi serve the agent everything? (No, it truncates)
articles.blurb: Every Pi tool is capped at 2,000 lines or 50 KB, whichever hits first. read keeps the head with offset/limit paging; bash keeps the tail and spills the full output to a temp file with a pointer; grep caps at 100 matches and 500-char lines; find at 1,000 results. Bounded by default, with an escape hatch.

articles.title: How Pi does subagents: three mechanisms, three scales
articles.blurb: Pi has three answers to 'one job too big for one context': the subagent tool spawns isolated pi subprocesses (single/parallel/chain) driven by markdown role files (scout/planner/worker/reviewer, each with its own tools + model); handoff starts a fresh focused session; the orchestrator drives a whole fleet of instances across machines. Read straight from the extension + orchestrator source.

articles.title: Surfaces: one headless core, four front-ends
articles.blurb: The same AgentSession core drives a full terminal UI, a one-shot print mode, a JSON mode, and a 100-command JSONL RPC protocol — plus an SDK you embed in your own app. How Pi separates the agent from the way you talk to it, and why that separation is the whole point of a harness.

articles.title: Extensibility: everything is an extension
articles.blurb: The last layer, and the one that makes Pi Pi. A single ExtensionAPI lets you register tools, slash commands, shortcuts, whole LLM providers, and hook ~31 lifecycle events (tool_call, context, session_before_compact…). Skills add progressive disclosure on top. How a small core becomes anything you need.
